# Scorched-Earth Erdős Sweep — Turn 71

This is turn 71. Turns 1 through 70 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL — use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 71.

## Turn 71 — rank–Dickson parity replaces the coupled-cycle casework

The candidate remains unchanged:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

As before, the proof uses the genuine profinite idempotent \(\omega _2\); \(40491355905\) is its required verifier residue. 

Turn 70 obtained hyperbolicity through a coupled-cycle Gauss-sum argument.  That argument can be replaced by a shorter invariant proof which automatically includes every scalar twist.

Let \((V,q)\) be a ramified self-dual simple tame \(\mathbf F _2\)-module, let \(B\) be the polar form of \(q\), and put

\[
U=S^{\omega _2}.
\]

The determinant obstruction produced by the actual \(A_2\) word is

\[
q_U(x)=q(x)+B(x,U^{-1}x).
\]

Its polar form is

\[
B_U(x,y)=B\bigl(x,(1+U+U^{-1})y\bigr).
\]

Since \(U\) has \(2\)-power order, \(1+U+U^{-1}\) is invertible: after multiplying by \(U\), this reduces to the coprimality of \(X^2+X+1\) and every power of \(X+1\).

### 1. The rank–Dickson identity is valid here

The objection raised in Turn 56 used a “singular transvection.” But if

\[
r_v(x)=x+B(x,v)v,
\]

then

\[
q(r_vx)+q(x)=B(x,v)\bigl(q(v)+1\bigr).
\]

Thus \(r_v\) is orthogonal precisely when \(q(v)=1\), not when \(q(v)=0\). On such an orthogonal symmetry,

\[
q_{r_v}(x)=q(x)+B(x,v),
\]

so the Arf invariant flips by \(1\), exactly matching
\(\operatorname{rank}(1+r_v)=1\).

Wall reduction therefore gives the general relative-Arf formula

\[
\operatorname{Arf}(q_U)
=
\operatorname{Arf}(q)
+
\operatorname{rank}(1+U)
\pmod 2.
\tag{71.1}
\]

Equivalently, the relevant Wall invariant is the ordinary Dickson invariant

\[
D(U)=\operatorname{rank}(1+U)\pmod2.
\]

So the earlier distinction between “rank parity” and the Dickson invariant was unnecessary in characteristic two.

### 2. Both terms in (71.1) equal the same multiplicity parity

Let \(I=\langle T\rangle\), and let \(W\) be the irreducible \(I\)-constituent of \(V\). By Clifford theory,

\[
V|_I\simeq W^{\oplus s}
\]

for some multiplicity \(s\). If the inertia character has order \(d\), write

\[
f=\operatorname{ord}_d(2)=2^a r,\qquad r\ \text{odd}.
\]

Ramified self-duality implies \(a\ge1\) and

\[
2^{f/2}\equiv-1\pmod d.
\]

Now

\[
D_I=\operatorname{End}_I(W)\simeq\mathbf F_{2^f}
\]

has involution \(z\mapsto z^{2^{f/2}}\). The restriction of \(q\) corresponds to a nonsingular Hermitian form on an \(s\)-dimensional multiplicity space over \(D_I\). Finite-field Hermitian diagonalization reduces it to \(s\) norm–trace lines. Every such line is minus type, so

\[
\operatorname{Arf}(q)\equiv s\pmod2.
\tag{71.2}
\]

For \(U=S^{\omega _2}\), the \(f\) inertia weights split into \(r\) cycles of length \(2^a\). Each weight has multiplicity \(s\). Moreover \(U^{2^a}\) centralizes both \(S\) and \(T\); it is a central \(2\)-element, hence acts trivially on the simple characteristic-two module \(V\). Therefore each \(U\)-cycle contributes an \(s\)-dimensional fixed space:

\[
\dim V^U=rs.
\]

Consequently,

\[
\operatorname{rank}(1+U)
=fs-rs
=rs(2^a-1)
\equiv s\pmod2.
\tag{71.3}
\]

Combining (71.1)–(71.3),

\[
\operatorname{Arf}(q_U)=s+s=0.
\]

Thus the candidate determinant obstruction is hyperbolic for every ramified self-dual simple tame module, including every coupled scalar-twist branch. No orbit-by-orbit residual-constant calculation is needed.

In the unramified case \(U=1\), so \(q_U=q\), and the norm–trace form remains minus type, as required.

### Exact finite audit

I also exhaustively enumerated all ramified irreducible self-dual tame matrix pairs in dimensions \(2\) and \(4\), together with every invariant nonsingular quadratic form:

| \(\dim V\) | \(\operatorname{ord}T\) | \(\operatorname{ord}S\) | marked instances | \(\operatorname{Arf}(q)\) | \(\operatorname{rank}(1+U)\) | \(\operatorname{Arf}(q_U)\) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 4 | 5 | 4 | 15 | 1 | 3 | 0 |
| 4 | 3 | 6 | 60 | 0 | 2 | 0 |

The final row is the first genuine scalar-twist branch. All \(78\) instances satisfy the rank–Dickson identity and give hyperbolic \(q_U\).

[Turn 71 exact certificate](sandbox:/mnt/data/turn71_dickson_rank_certificate.py)

**ESTABLISHED**: Replaced Turn 70’s coupled-cycle Gauss-sum casework by a uniform Wall–Dickson argument. Corrected the false transvection objection from Turn 56. For every ramified self-dual simple tame module, \(\operatorname{Arf}(q)\equiv\operatorname{rank}(1+S^{\omega _2})\), so the actual \(A_2\) determinant form is hyperbolic. Exhaustively verified all \(78\) marked instances in dimensions \(2\) and \(4\), including the scalar-twist branch.

**GAP**: The private 5402-group verifier has not been run. The full decorated marked induction and local Kummer theorem still need consolidation into one formal proof note.

**NEXT**: Replace the coupled-cycle section of the proof with the multiplicity-parity theorem above, then assemble the determinant lemma with the marked chief-factor induction and submit the unchanged residue block.
